2: The community that gathers at Adullam is described in three categories: those in māṣôq (distress/straits), those with a creditor (nāšāʾ), and the mar nepeš (bitter of soul/desperate). This is the social portrait of the rejected and marginalized — those for whom the existing order has no place, gathered around the rejected anointed. David's Adullam community is the narrative type of the community Jesus gathers: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28); the beatitudes of Matthew 5:3-6 address precisely the māṣôq, the nāšāʾ, the mar nepeš — the poor in spirit, those who mourn, the meek, those who hunger. As David at Adullam was not yet on the throne but was already the king-in-hiding who drew the outcasts to himself, so Christ in his earthly ministry is the king-in-exile gathering those whom the existing religious order has excluded, building the community of the coming kingdom before its public establishment.
20: Abiathar's escape with the ephod from the Nob massacre creates the typological connection between the slaughter of the priestly community and the preservation of divine access for the fugitive anointed. The Nob massacre — Doeg killing 85 priests of YHWH — is the most violent act of the pre-exilic narrative against the sacred ministers of YHWH. Yet in its midst, the one who survives brings the means of covenantal inquiry to David. Matthew draws the explicit typological parallel in 1 Samuel 2:16-18: Herod's slaughter of the innocents around the newly revealed king mirrors the Nob massacre, and Rachel weeping for her children (Jeremiah 31:15) is the mourning that accompanies the birth of the anointed. In both cases, a massacre around the anointed king results in the preservation of those who serve the king's covenantal access — Abiathar with the ephod (the means of priestly inquiry) as the type of the Spirit who opens the way into the presence of God (Hebrews 10:19-22: access through the blood of Jesus, through the new and living way).
1: David's flight from Gath (1 Samuel 21) brings him to the cave of Adullam: wayyāḇōʾ dāwid šammāh wayyiśmᵉʿû ʾeḥāyw wᵉḵol bêt ʾāḇîw wayyērᵉdû ʾēlāyw šammāh — his brothers and all his father's household come down to him. The rejected anointed becomes the gathering-point for his family — those who had overlooked him (cf. 1 Samuel 16:11) now shelter with him. Adullam was a cave in the Shephelah near the Philistine border — liminal territory between Israel and its enemies. The cave as hideout of the anointed-in-exile becomes a recurrent type: Elijah at the Horeb cave (1 Kings 19:9), the cave-dwelling saints of Hebrews 11:38, and ultimately the garden-tomb from which the risen Christ emerges as the Anointed-no-longer-hidden.
3: David leaves his parents at Mizpeh of Moab with the king: yēṣeʾ ʾāḇî wᵉʾimmî nāʾ ʾittᵉḵem ʿad ʾăšer ʾēdaʿ mah-yāʿăśeh lî ʾĕlōhîm — let my father and mother come out with you until I know what God will do for me. The deposit of his parents in Moab — the homeland of his great-grandmother Ruth — is both practical and symbolic: David preserves the family line by removing it from Saul's reach. The uncertainty expressed (ʿad ʾăšer ʾēdaʿ, until I know) models dependence on divine disclosure rather than human planning. Jesus's similar entrusting of his mother to the Beloved Disciple from the cross (John 19:26-27) has the same protective logic.
4: David's parents stay with the king of Moab all the days David was in the stronghold (bammᵉṣûdāh). The Moabite interlude recalls David's ancestry (Ruth 4:22) and demonstrates that YHWH's providential care for the messianic line runs through unlikely nations. The exile of the anointed king's family among foreign peoples — preserved and then restored — becomes part of the broader pattern of messianic preservation through hostile territory.
5: The prophet Gad instructs David: lōʾ tēšeḇ baṣṣᵉdāh lēḵ ûḇāʾtā lᵉḵā ʾereṣ yᵉhûdāh — do not remain in the stronghold; go into the land of Judah. The prophetic guidance directs the anointed king back into his own territory despite the danger. This tension — divine command to return to the dangerous homeland — is the pattern of the messianic mission: he came to his own, and his own did not receive him (John 1:11). David's obedience to prophetic direction despite personal risk foreshadows Jesus's willingness to enter Jerusalem knowing what awaited him.
6:
Saul hears that David and his men have been discovered: wayyēšeḇ baGiḇʿāh taḥat hāʾēšel bāRāmāh wᵉḥănîtô bᵉyādô wᵉḵol ʿăḇādāyw niṣṣāḇîm ʿālāyw

— he sits at Gibeah under the tamarisk tree with his spear in hand and his officials standing around him. The spear in Saul's hand is his signature symbol throughout Samuel — it appears at moments of murderous rage against David (1 Samuel 18:11; 1 Samuel 19:9) and becomes a metonym for the persecutor king. The rejected king's pursuer sits armed and surrounded by courtiers; the true king camps in wilderness caves. The power differential is visible and overwhelming.
7: Saul's speech to his Benjaminite officials: šimᵉʿû nāʾ bᵉnê Yᵉmînî gam-lᵉḵulleḵem yittēn ben-Yišay śādôt ûkᵉrāmîm lᵉḵulleḵem yāśîm śārê ʾălāpîm wᵉśārê mēʾôt — will the son of Jesse give you all fields and vineyards? Will he make you commanders? The appeal to tribal self-interest — patronage from the ruling house — is Saul's mechanism for loyalty. He frames David as a threat to Benjaminite privilege. The demagogue's appeal to material self-interest as a substitute for justice recurs throughout sacred history.
8: Saul accuses his men of conspiracy (qᵉšarttem ʿālay): waʾên gōleh ʾet ʾāznî bᵉḵaret bᵉnî ʿim ben Yišay — not one of you has informed me that my son Jonathan has made a covenant with the son of Jesse. The rage at Jonathan's covenant with David — the heir making a personal alliance with the rival — expresses Saul's deepest political fear. Jonathan's covenant is in fact the prophetic reality: the true king and the yielding heir have made their peace before the transfer of power happens.
9: Doeg the Edomite (cf. 1 Samuel 21:7) speaks up: rāʾîtî ʾet ben Yišay bāʾ Nōḇāh ʾel ʾAḥîmelek ben ʾĂḥîṭûḇ — I saw the son of Jesse come to Ahimelech at Nob. Doeg's denunciation is technically accurate but strategically treacherous. He was present when David received the bread and the sword (1 Samuel 21:7); his silence then enabled David's provision; his speech now enables the massacre. The informer who reports truth for murderous ends embodies a type of betrayal — accurate testimony used to destroy the innocent — that recurs in the NT: the witnesses at Jesus's trial (Mark 14:57-59) who quote his words in distorted context.
10: Doeg reports Ahimelech's actions: the priest inquired of YHWH for David (wayyišʾal lô baYHWH), gave him provisions, and gave him the sword of Goliath. The three acts — prophetic inquiry, provision of bread, provision of the weapon — correspond exactly to the priestly functions David sought (1 Samuel 21:2-9). Ahimelech had been deceived by David's false claim of a royal mission but had acted in good faith. Doeg's report transforms acts of priestly hospitality into evidence of treason.
11: Saul summons Ahimelech and all his father's house — the entire priestly family of Nob (kol bêt ʾāḇîw hakkōhănîm ʾăšer bᵉNōḇ). The summoning of the entire priestly community to stand before the king foreshadows the kangaroo-court dynamic: Saul has decided on the verdict before hearing the case. The power of the state arrayed against the priestly community for complicity with the rejected anointed is the structural parallel to the Sanhedrin gathering against Jesus and his followers.
12: Saul interrogates Ahimelech: šmaʿ nāʾ ben ʾĂḥîṭûḇ — hear now, son of Ahitub. wayyōʾmer hinnēnî ʾădōnî — and he said, here I am, my lord. The priest's readiness to answer recalls the prophetic vocation summons (hinnēnî, here I am — cf. Samuel's response to YHWH in 1 Samuel 3:4). Ahimelech presents himself openly to power. His willingness to stand before the hostile king contrasts with the witnesses who fled — the pattern of those who do not fear the earthly power because they serve a higher one.
13: Saul accuses Ahimelech: lāmmāh qᵉšarttem ʿālay ʾattāh ûḇen Yišay lātît lᵉḵā leḥem wᵉḥereḇ — why have you conspired against me, you and the son of Jesse, by giving him bread and a sword? The charge of conspiracy (qāšar) is the same applied to Jonathan's covenant (v8). Saul sees every relationship that does not serve his power as treason. The priest's priestly act — giving the bread of the Presence and a weapon to someone he believed was on royal business — is reframed as military conspiracy.
14: Ahimelech's defense: ûmî ḇᵉḵol ʿăḇādêḵā kaḏāwid neʾĕmān wᵉḥātān hammélek wᵉsār ʾel mišmaʿtêḵā — who among all your servants is as faithful as David, the king's son-in-law, captain of your bodyguard? Ahimelech argues from David's evident loyalty — his status as royal son-in-law, captain. The defense appeals to David's track record rather than to priestly law. The priest does not know David has been condemned; he is defending a man on the basis of that man's character and prior relationship with the king.
15: hāyôm hiḥillōtî lišʾol lô bēlōhîm ḥālîlāh lî — was today the first time I inquired of God for him? Far from it! Ahimelech protests his ongoing priestly service to David. He concludes: ʾal yāśem hammélek bᵉʿaḇdô dāḇār bᵉḵol bêt ʾāḇî kî lōʾ yādaʿ ʿaḇdᵉḵā bᵉḵol zōʾt dāḇār qāṭōn wᵉgādôl — do not impute anything to your servant or to all my father's house, for your servant knew nothing of all this, small or great. The priestly innocence-claim before the king parallels Jesus's silence before Pilate (Matthew 27:14) — the innocent refusing to defend themselves against unjust power while still making clear their integrity.
16: Saul's verdict: môt tāmût ʾAḥîmelek ʾattāh wᵉḵol bêt ʾāḇîḵā — you shall surely die, Ahimelech, you and all your father's house. The death sentence on the entire priestly household is collective punishment: guilt-by-association extended to family. The legal structure of Israel prohibited collective punishment (Deuteronomy 24:16), but Saul's paranoid monarchy overrides law. The condemned priest community parallels the early Christian martyrs condemned by state authority for association with the true king.
17: Saul orders his guards (rāṣîm, runners) to kill the priests of YHWH: pᵉnû wᵉhāmîtû kōhănê YHWH kî gam yādām ʿim dāwid — because their hand also is with David. The guards refuse (lōʾ ʾāḇû ʿăḇadê hammélek lišlōaḥ ʾet yādām lipgōʿ bᵉḵōhănê YHWH) — they would not stretch out their hand against the priests of YHWH. The guards' refusal is a sign of remaining moral conscience even within the apostate court. There is always a remnant who will not cross the line of sacrilege, even under direct royal command.
18: Doeg the Edomite carries out the massacre: wayyāsob Dōʾeg hāʾĕdōmî wayyipgaʿ hûʾ bakkōhănîm wayyāmot bayyôm hahûʾ šᵉmōnîm wᵉḥămiššāh ʾîš nōśēʾ ʾēpôd bād — Doeg the Edomite turned and fell upon the priests, eighty-five men who wore the linen ephod. The specific detail — eighty-five priests wearing the ephod — emphasizes that these were serving ministers slaughtered in priestly garb. The ephod (sign of their office) does not protect them; the murderer's foreignness (Edomite) contrasts with the Israelite guards' refusal. The outsider does what no Israelite was willing to do. The slaughter of the priestly community nearest to the fugitive anointed echoes in Matthew's Herod narrative: the innocents slaughtered near the infant messianic king (Matthew 2:16-18) follow the Adullam-and-Nob structural pattern.
19: wᵉʾet Nōḇ ʿîr hakkōhănîm hikkāh leḥad haḥereḇ mēʾîš wᵉʿad ʾiššāh mēʿôlēl wᵉʿad yônēq ûšôr waḥămôr wᵉśeh leḥad haḥereḇ — and Nob, the city of priests, he struck with the edge of the sword, both men and women, children and nursing infants, oxen, donkeys, and sheep — with the edge of the sword. The totality of the destruction — applying the ḥerem-like formula to a priestly city — inverts the holy-war command: Saul failed to apply this formula to Amalek (1 Samuel 15) as commanded and now applies it to YHWH's own priests. The persecutor's violence, misdirected and self-serving, destroys the holy community while sparing the enemy.
21: Abiathar tells David that Saul has killed the priests of YHWH. David's response in v22 takes responsibility. This verse establishes the connection: the only surviving priest, carrying the ephod, finds refuge with the rejected anointed. Abiathar's flight to David creates the structural fulfillment of priestly and royal alignment — the legitimate heir of the priestly line joins the legitimate heir of the royal line, reconstituting the proper order in exile.
22: wayyōʾmer dāwid lᵉʾAḇyātār yādaʿtî bayyôm hahûʾ kî šam Dōʾeg hāʾĕdōmî kî haggēd yaggîd lᵉŠāʾûl ʾānōḵî sabhōtî bᵉḵol nepeš bêt ʾāḇîḵā — I knew on that day when Doeg the Edomite was there that he would surely tell Saul. I have caused the death of all the persons of your father's house. David's self-accusation — ʾānōḵî sabbōtî, I have turned about, caused this — is a remarkable acceptance of moral responsibility for consequences he did not directly cause. His deception of Ahimelech (1 Samuel 21:2) triggered the chain; he owns that. This willingness to bear responsibility for the innocent deaths that resulted from his decisions points to the pattern fully expressed in Christ: he bore our sins in his body on the tree (1 Peter 2:24). The rejected anointed takes on the weight of deaths he did not intend but whose chain he initiated.
23: šeḇ ʾittî ʾal tîrāʾ kî ʾăšer yᵉḇaqqēš ʾet napšî yᵉḇaqqēš ʾet napšeḵā kî mišmeret ʾattāh ʿimmādî — stay with me, do not fear; for whoever seeks my life seeks yours also; with me you shall be in safekeeping. David offers the survivor of the Nob massacre his own protection — the fugitive king sheltering the refugee priest. mišmeret (safekeeping, guard) is the same word used for the priestly vigil over the tabernacle (Numbers 3:28). The king's protection of the priest reverses the royal-priestly relationship: normally the king relies on the priest's intercession; here the king shelters the priest in mortal danger.
